[IMAGE:0]It's a little before midnight, so we can proceed with a new Magic Monday. Ask me anything about occultism and I'll do my best to answer it. With certain exceptions, any question received by midnight Monday Eastern time will get an answer. Please note:  Any question or comment received after then will not get an answer, and in fact will just be deleted. (I've been getting an increasing number of people trying to post after these are closed, so will have to draw a harder line than before.) If you're in a hurry, or suspect you may be the 143,916th person to ask a question, please check out the very rough version 1.1 of The Magic Monday FAQ here. Also: I will not be putting through or answering any more questions about practicing magic around children. I've answered those in simple declarative sentences in the FAQ. If you read the FAQ and don't think your question has been answered, read it again. If that doesn't help, consider remedial reading classes; yes, it really is as simple and straightforward as the FAQ says. 

The image? I field a lot of questions about my books these days, so I've decided to do little capsule summaries of them here, one per week. The book above on the left is my eighth published book, Learning Ritual Magic. This is the first project I did with other authors, and it came about in a somewhat roundabout way.  In the years just before this appeared I was involved in founding and running a magical lodge in the Seattle area, and the lodge had a study course for prospective members to work through.  When the lodge folded -- yeah, it was the standard political problems that afflict such groups, which I hadn't yet learned how to forestall -- the three of us who were senior members, who'd written the course, decided to publish it as a book. It turned out to be a solid introduction to Golden Dawn-style ritual magic, and makes a good first step before tackling Circles of Power or any other more advanced test.  If you're interested in the book, you can get a copy here if you live in the US, and at your favorite book venue if you live elsewhere.

replies.content:
Cooking professionals of my acquaintance swear by the 5-element-process from Traditional Chinese Medicine. They assure that not only the fact of incorporating ingredients for each of the 5 taste qualities is important, but that it really helps to do it in the proper order according to the feeding cycle of elements (wood/sour/fresh - fire/bitter/smoky - earth/sweet/plain - metal/spicy/dry - water/salty/brined).

Usually, fire from the stove is your starting point, then oil in the pan for earth, neutral (=earthy) vegetables, then onion and/or spices for metal, salt/sardine/seaweed/water, fresh leaves/tomato/vinegar for wood, then other (bitter) spices or leaves (most mediterranean ones like basil, thyme, rosemary, paprika or turmeric) and you have a full cycle. Stir after every element and go forth in the cycle as long as you need, end anywhere you like.

Most recipes can be adapted to this process, as all kinds of meat, cheese, alcohols, vegetables and other ingredients have their elemental attributions which are published in lists like this one: http://5elementfood.blogspot.com/p/five-elements-of-food.html

I've been using this for many years and find it successful, and according to our host it is a kind of enchantment - treating the food you cook like a process of life, which, according to chinese philosophy, goes through the cycle of the five elements all the time!

Dear John Michael Greer,

1) In the CGD Taranis is the Bull god of the Earth but in The Litany, he is the god of the Heavens, but I have also seen him as a human looking god of thunder that is depicted with lightning in his hand along with a wheel. Could you help clear this up for me?

2) Do you agree the work of Iolo Morganwg are forgeries, and if so, how could one person come up with all this epic mythology?

3) This brings me to something I am trying to understand, of what is “real” and what is symbols of something real: For example, Hu Gadarn is a character from Morganwg’s work which I am assuming he made up or based him off something else such as Charlamagne, so when he is invoked, or even if the deity of Chokmah is invoked, is the idea that these are basically ways to get our minds to go beneath these deities into this masculine force, but the deity itself are just tools, not real entities, just tools to guide our minds to something real? Same in the Lesser Ritual be it an archangel like Raphael or the Hawk of May, the idea is these are tools to guide us to what is beneath them and this may be the point of many of the world’s religious deities? Is this correct?

4) Finally, in the Litany, do you see any problem with me snapping my fingers in place of ringing the bell?

Thank you for your time!
Jeremy

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
1) Nope. Divine imagery routinely varies this way, because gods are transrational -- meaning that they don't fit within the cubbyholes we like to think with. Apollo, for example, was a healing god but also a sun god and the patron god of mice (Apollo Smintheus); there were temples of Aphrodite where her statue had a beard, and the list goes on.

2) I've discussed the complexities of Iolo's sources in an essay, pp. 19-26 in this publication:

http://aoda.org/trilithon/TrilithonVol1_Web.pdf

The short form is that his mythology includes things that nobody in his time could have invented, because they contain accurate references to archaic Indo-European mythologems at a time when comparative mythology was in his infancy. He invented a lot, but he also drew on a lot of ancient material.

3) In the strict sense everything we experience is a symbol; we have no direct access to reality at all, just to reflections of it constructed in our minds out of the raw material provided us by our senses and our memories. All names and all images are symbols -- and "by names and images are all powers awakened and reawakened." Thus it doesn't matter diddley-squat where a given name or image comes from; what matters is whether you can invoke a living power by calling on it. But the symbols are not merely tools. They're interfaces -- we interact them, and so do the unimaginable realities that contact us through them.

4) Why not simply get a small bell or chime instead?

replies.title: Taranis
replies.content:
About the question of how Taranis could be God of thunder and sky, and also be the Earth Bull...

I don't work with Taranis and I'm not familiar with the reference, but I do work with Thor, who's a God of thunder and lightning, but also the son of the Earth Goddess, and a God of farmers and others who work on practical, down to earth labors.

But the most relevant perception I have about this question applies to Taranis and Poseidon as well as Thor: on certain levels of perception and relationship we can understand the Deities (and ourselves) as vibrations and resonances of energies. Thor and the others are very powerful 'vibrators' if you will--the motion of their beings when they are intensely active sets vibrations rolling across various Elements, Planes, and Worlds. It's no surprise that their vibrations move through the Sea and the Earth as well as the Sky, and impact all of them.

These guys are not limited by space or power or human ideas about their forms and functions, and it's not a good idea to suggest that they are! As humans, we can try our best to comprehend them, but different people will grasp different aspects of the Deities' natures, so its useful to listen to others and compare notes.

Winifred Hodge Rose

replies.title: Re: The Soul of the Knees continued
replies.content:
Jill, thanks for this interesting post and your kind words! There is a lot in ancient (pre-Socratic) Greek beliefs that touches on interfaces between our souls and our bodies, and this is something I also work on with my Heathen soul lore books, published and in progress. There's so much about that in folklore, as well. Some of it is hilarious, like the old Swedish folklore idea of the nasehug or nose-Hugr. Hugr is a very energetic soul in Germanic Heathen thought, which can easily exit the body and get up to all kinds of stuff. One of the things it can do is exit a person who is going to visit someone else, fly off to the host and up the host's nose, making the nose itch and sneeze. Thus, the host knows that a visitor is coming, thanks to the 'nasehug'! :-D I have to say, though, that my own Hugr does not find this idea very appealing!

One of the many interesting things about Hugr is its similarity to the 'soul of the liver', the Hun, in Chinese medicine, as well as its similarity to the ancient Greek Thymos soul. Their characteristics are similar, but their locations are slightly different: Hun in the liver, Thymos in the thymus gland, and Hugr in the heart.

As you say, we all have complex energies circulating in us, and it's no surprise if cultures which are very different and not in contact with each other still end up perceiving similar phenomena, but with variations, with regard to human nature on all levels. For example, in Chinese poetic speech they may refer to someone dear to them as "my liver," the way we in the West would call such a person "my heart," while in Norse poetry it is the Hugr, specifically, who loves and deeply knows the beloved person. And Hugr happens to hang out in the chest and around the heart, but that's very close to the liver, too. When Hugr characteristically 'swells with power' within the chest, it affects all the organs of the torso. It's really the soul-function they're all referring to, and that soul-function may be rooted in somewhat different anatomical locations based on cultural understandings of our physical organs, systems, and their functions.

In my work with Heathen souls I spend a lot of exploratory effort (research effort and personal exploration) on what I call the 'footholds' of our souls within our physical body and its systems. It's something I've written about in my two Heathen soul lore books to date, and am working more on for future books and posting on my website. There's a lot to explore and learn here!

I'm currently reading through JMG's "Secret of the 5 Rites" to see what other interesting inter-cultural overlaps of 'soul-footholds' I might find, other than the 'soul of the knees.'

I'll join you in thanking JMG and everyone on the list here; even when I don't respond I really enjoy all the posts and discussions, and learn a lot!

Winifred Hodge Rose

comments.title: The Persistence of Memory
comments.content:
Hello Archdruid,

This week I have two questions:

1) My first question involves the Opening Gesture taught in Learning Ritual Magic which I have been practicing throughout the course; I am in Lesson 5 for context. My question is less about the act of Opening itself and more a phenomenon that has occurred to me. I did something embarrassing a little over a week or so ago. Normally I would forget and move on from this like any other embarrassing memory one experiences. However, I remembered this embarrassing memory while doing the Opening Gesture during my daily practice and each time I open I now think of this embarrassing memory. I will go the whole day without thinking of it but the moment I Open or think about Opening or my magical practice in general, there it is. It has become somewhat distracting during my practice as well as I have just been freshly reminded of it while doing the relaxation and attention exercises, meditating, or doing ritual. I also want to clarify that although the event was embarrassing or maybe shameful, I am not emotionally perturbed by its remembrance, I just find it to be distracting during my practice.
So my question is are there any strategies to disentangle a memory from the Opening gesture? I am almost wary of continuing practicing despite the benefits I have already felt from my daily practice because I don't want to deepen the association of the memory with the gesture. short of quitting for a time I am at a loss for what to do which brings me to my next question which may contain the solution...

2) My second question is whether it is okay to cease all practice for a period of about two weeks and what results might I expect from such a decision? Regardless of the presence of the memory or not, I probably need to cease my practice around the holidays because I will be traveling with, and surrounded by, family for the duration. They would not be sympathetic to the practice of magic and I cannot reliably disappear for an hour or so each day to practice given travel and lodging arrangements. So is it alright to cease practicing for this time, and what should I do once normalcy returns to reengage my practice?

Thanks and best regards.

replies.title: Re: The Persistence of Memory
replies.content:
1) Your lower mind is bringing that up to try to interfere with your practice. Accept the memory and then put it aside and go on with your practice.

2) It's not good to stop completely. Can you take things down to ten minutes of absolutely essential practice and do that in the bathroom or something?

replies.title: Re: The Persistence of Memory
replies.content:
Thanks for the tip on memory, it definitely feels like those things that come up during meditation to distract you, only it's at the beginning of practice. I will try to put it aside as you say.

I suppose I can find a way to do the relaxation, attention, will, and maybe the Tarot meditation exercises but the ritual is going to be difficult, unless you think that can be done in the bathroom? It's the vibration of Divine names or other phrases that seems the biggest obstacle as I usually half to annunciate on the louder side to "feel" the vibration in the relevant bodily center. I will try though to annunciate quieter and perhaps more deeply.

Also just to clarify, what would the absolutely essential elements of practice be for you?

Thanks again.

2) For me, the non-negotiable daily practices are a basic daily protective ritual (Pentagram ritual, Sphere of Protection, or what have you), a daily meditation, and a daily divination.

Hello JMG. Samat here again.

1. I've seen some discussions in the geomantic circles that your borrowing from Cattan's idea of 'nigromancy' derived from the eight house shouldn't mean that all questions related to magical workings belong to this house. Nick Farrell postulates that the 8th corresponds to modern astrology and the 9th corresponds to magick and that is what he uses. Another said to look at the entire chart and not just the 8th house. A third told me that while 8th is the house of magick, 9th is the house of communication with 'higher spirits'.

In your experience and opinion, are all magickal queries accurately direction to the 8th house?

For example, I rolled the dice and asked, 'Will I be able to communicate with a specific angel of Capricorn today through pathworking?' and the people interpreted the chart to show unfavor because the 8th house had FMajor - a highly unfavorable sign for this house - and the court had Carcer as the judge and Via and Conjunctio as left and right witnesses respectively.

3. Finally, do you recommend casting a reading after the said conversation took place to ensure whether I was speaking to my imagination or actually the angel itself?

replies.title: Re: Painful Geomancy
replies.content:
1) I assign all ritual magic done by the querent to the 8th. Divination of all kinds, including astrology, belongs to the 9th. However, your mileage may vary, and you'll want to compare your interpretations to what actually happened in each situation and judge from that.

2) I use the 8th house for that.

3) I've never found that useful.

Psychology is shaped by one's family influences to an extent, so that the conscious psychological effect of the name change would have been strengthened by occult influences from the ancestors and the families' respective orlog, wyrd, and hamingja. Names, including surnames, are obviously linked to multigenerational influences; that's how the Norse tried to ensure reincarnation, or the continuation of a person's hamingja, in the family line by giving the name of the deceased to a baby. Hamingja, in a way, encompasses all of these influences together, past and present, and fastens threads of wyrd, spun from the past, to our present deeds and intentions that tug us gently or strongly in specific future directions.

Winifred Hodge Rose

To begin with, candle magic is fairly simple stuff. replies.title: Re: Dizzyingly intense blessing walk
replies.content:
Dear 'Dizzy',

I've had similar experiences, and after years of puzzling I figured out at least one factor. I'm very motivated toward 'saving' and 'helping' the environment and entities of the environment, directed my whole career toward that. I finally realized that my focus was very one-way: me 'helping / blessing / saving' them, or trying to. It blocked me from seeing what a two-way street that really is. Failing to recognize that reciprocity ended up, for me at least, messing up the proper flow of energy between us. Even though I knew there is a two-way flow, it was unbalanced and roiled in me and created that fatigue and dizziness. Since then I've tried it in reverse: gratefully accepting their blessings first, then giving a moderated blessing in return and thanks. Somehow, by recognizing their leadership in this process, I've made it less overwhelming for myself.

By the way, you might be interested to know that our word 'dizzy' comes from Proto-Indo-European *dhues, *dhuesmi, "to breathe, to be full of wild spirits." It's related to words for dizzy, ghost / spirit, wild animal, confusion, rage, in various Indo-European languages. I think the concept is very much related to shamanic practice. (This is from Mallory & Adams "Encyclopedia of Indo-European Culture.") I struggle with constant dizziness myself, and I think some of it comes from energies and communication from physical and spiritual entities that I'm not able to fully manage and moderate, though there are physical reasons for my dizziness as well.

Winifred Hodge Rose

comments.title: Exorcism
comments.content:
Greetings JMG and commentariat,

How might one go about finding an exorcist? I know I could reach out to the Catholic Church, but I’m wondering about other options.

Also, if a non-Christian received an exorcism from a Christian exorcist, would that matter? Would this tie the recipient to the Christian faith in some way, or have some other noteworthy effect?

replies.title: Re: Exorcism
replies.content: Eastern Orthodox priests exorcise everything, routinely (our baptisms and annual house-blessings include an exorcism). Not sure if that's a service they offer to non-Orthodox, but it wouldn't hurt to ask. The worst they can say is "no".

replies.title: Re: Exorcism
replies.content: You might see if you can find a nearby non-Roman branch of the Catholic church. Many of their clergy are very good exorcists, and they're less likely to push their faith on you.

replies.title: Re: Exorcism
replies.content: I’m wondering if - since Yahweh is a jealous god - he might expect some covenant on my behalf. Is this one of those questions best settled by prayer?

replies.title: Re: Exorcism
replies.content: No, he won't.  Both major and minor exorcisms are sacramentals in the Catholic Church.  Which means they're a blessing conferred via a particular rite that is established by the Church's authority.  The covenant you speak of is taken up with baptism, and later reaffirmed via confirmation.

replies.title: Re: Exorcism
replies.content:
I'd suggest an Eastern Orthodox church is an option. They have extremely competent, and very well trained exorcists, and they are usually also trained to try to help with the non-spiritual issues (ex: checking the area for gas leaks, getting a psychiatrist to assess the person), and if they see something they can't handle they have the ability to call on members of the church who do; and since it is considered far worse for someone to join the church and leave than for someone to never join at all, they consider it a serious sin to try to use crisis to push their faith on someone.

The one case I know where someone actually got an exorcist from one of them, the priest outright told them not to attend a mass for a few weeks minimum. They were extremely grateful and eager to join, but he was adamant that they needed time to think and assess things before even considering trying to join the church, so if you are worried about that kind of pressure during a crisis, this is an option.

replies.title: Re: Exorcism
replies.content:
From the Roman Catholic perspective, only baptism (and later confirmation) would so bind you to the Catholic deity or the Church. Non-Catholic Christians and non-Christians can receive exorcism if their situation calls for it. From the United States Conference of Catholic Bishops:

"May anyone receive a 'major exorcism?'

Since the rites of exorcism are categorized as sacramentals, effectively as blessings, the practice of who may receive a 'major exorcism' is governed by canon 1170 of the Code of Canon Law. The following are able to receive this specialized blessing if it is determined necessary: 1) Catholics; 2) Catechumens; 3) Non-Catholic Christians who request it; and 4) Non-Christian believers provided they have the proper disposition—meaning, they are sincere in their desire to be free of demonic influence. In cases involving a non-Catholic, the matter should be brought to the attention of the Diocesan Bishop (cf. ERS, no. 18)."

Additional information can be found here: https://www.usccb.org/prayer-and-worship/sacraments-and-sacramentals/sacramentals-blessings/exorcism

If you can't find a non-Catholic exorcist near you, and you are comfortable with receiving a Catholic sacramental (that is what exorcism is), you can contact the local parish priest or the diocese's main office to get the process started.

Also worth considering is a particular independent Catholic bishop, Agostino Taumaturgo. He didn't provide me with an exorcism (though he does perform them) but he did provide me with several prayers that were incredibly effective at getting rid of a curse that was sent my way.

replies.title: Re: Exorcism
replies.content:
I’ve conversed with him by e-mail. Very nice man.

—Princess Cutekitten

replies.title: Re: Exorcism
replies.content: That he is.  He and JMG are my two primary sources on magic and the occult.

Not exactly what you're asking, but one of the main models for D&D magic (at least the kind wizards use) was the magic in Jack Vance's stories (mostly the "Dying Earth" ones, but the Lyonesse books follow similar principles). The idea that a spell was something you stuffed into your head through specialized study and that left your mind on being cast was both evocative and made for good gameplay by making spells a resource that could be managed strategically (use the powerful spell now, or save it in case something more dangerous comes along?).

Cheers,
Jeff

3) The previous age of Saturn seems to have been known as a mythical Golden age in the far-flung past by the ancient Greeks and Romans, yet the age of Saturn seems to also be marked by hardship, misery and restriction. Aquarius is a Saturn/Uranus sign, but Saturn is associated with lead, not gold (Uranus doesn't appear to have a metal, as far as I can tell) - what was it about the Saturnian age which made the Greeks think it was so golden?

And as a final aside on the above - I don't know about you, but I've already noticed that karma seems to have been turbocharged in the last few years (since the grand mutation in 2020).

Thanks.
Mr. Crow

replies.title: Re: Astrological ages
replies.content:
Also, as an odd synchronicity follow-up to this - about an hour after I posted the above message, the next podcast on my queue happened to be all about the transition to the age of Aquarius (and my podcasts were on random shuffle, plus the title didn't even make it obvious that was the subject of the episode)

I've generally had a huge number of synchronicities in the past few years (I note them down) and my instinctive sense has gone crazy in the past few months - I will think about something, and then have something related happen within hours, or even minutes. Is there any way to harness/develop this ability?

Thanks.
Mr. Crow

replies.title: Re: Astrological ages
replies.content:
1) Aquarius is governed by Uranus, a malefic planet that rules sudden changes, disasters, individuality, eccentricity, independence, fanaticism, blind impulses, revolutions, and innovations. The age of Aquarius will thus feature many crises and sudden changes, and the dissolution of social unities into smaller, more eccentric groups. The religions that thrive during that period will be those that leave ample room for charismatic visions and individual inspirations; the political history of the age will be littered with sudden catastrophes and the abrupt collapse of nations; the intellectual scene will be short on consensus and very long on dissidence.

2) The exact date at which each age begins is a subject of constant contention. My working guess is that the first significant Piscean influence came through around 270 BC -- but yes, there was a long ragged transition sprawled out over centuries.

3) That's one of the great mysteries of the symbolism. The age assigned to Saturn by the classical writers was the golden age, which was the age of Leo -- in Hesiod's scheme, Leo is the golden age, Cancer the silver age, Gemini the bronze age (note the mixture of two metals), Taurus the age of heroes (cf. Mithras and The Cattle Raid of Cooley), and Aries the age of iron. (Pisces, to take it a step further with the help of Daniel, is the age of clay.) Yet leaden Saturn is assigned to the golden age also -- and there's the strange old claim in occult writings that Saturn is in some sense "the old Sun." What does it mean? I don't claim to know.

comments.title: Qs
comments.content:
1) Dear JMG, since you've had past lives memories, have you be able to figure out some of the repeating characters in your life?

2) Similar, you've said we get close to some level of free will as we come close to finishing our material lives, including picking our next incarnation. Im presuming this would apply to your recent lifetimes? If a particular lifetime was very hard or action packed, is one able to choose an easier one?

3) Does the idea that time doesn't work on higher planes indicate that Nietzsche's eternal return idea is literally true in some way? And that all lives of a soul are co-occuring on higher planes?

replies.title: Re: Qs
replies.content:
1) Yes, I know some of the repeating cast fairly well at this point.

2) To the extent that karma permits, yes. "Some level of free will" doesn't mean complete free will -- it can mean no more than a little wiggle room.

3) Nietzsche's idea only works to the extent that all times are equally present in eternity. That doesn't mean that they repeat, just that they're all there at once. Think about the way when you were a very small child, you had to spell out words one letter at a time; C, A, T.
Later, you could see them all at once at the same moment: cat. The first is like being in time, the second like being in eternity.

replies.title: Re: Qs
replies.content:
Correct, so all lives are co-occurant at the higher levels (presuming some higher property we don't understand yet would explain/replace the ordinality that cat is still subject to inside time-space).

So the idea that we are moving on to other more interesting things when we shed the material body is a concept that is applicable on lower planes, not the eternal one.

replies.title: Re: Qs
replies.content: Yes. From the perspective of eternity, we are at once all the forms of our incarnations, from single-celled organisms up to human forms, and also all those expressions of ourselves beyond incarnation that, in time, we haven't reached yet.
